blocks.html: III Cohomological invariants and depth

pages.title: Reminders
pages.html:
1 Reminders

We shall state some definitions and results that the reader will find, for example, in chapter I of the course given by J.P. SERRE at the Collège de France in 1957-58.1

Definition 1.1

Let \(A\) be a ring (commutative with unit element, as throughout the following) and let \(M\) be an \(A\)-module (unitary, as throughout the following); one calls:

  • annihilator of \(M\), denoted \(\operatorname{Ann} M\), the set of \(a\in A\) such that for all \(m\in M\) one has \(am=0\).

  • support of \(M\), denoted \(\operatorname{Supp} M\), the set of prime ideals \(\mathfrak{p} \) of \(A\) such that the localization \(M_\mathfrak{p}\) is non-zero.

  • "assassin of \(M\)" or "set of prime ideals associated to \(M\)", denoted \(\operatorname{Ass} M\), the set of prime ideals \(\mathfrak{p} \) of \(A\) such that there exists a non-zero element of \(M\) whose annihilator is \(\mathfrak{p} \).

If \(\mathfrak{a}\) is an ideal of \(A\), we shall denote by \(\mathfrak{r}(\mathfrak{a})\) the radical of \(\mathfrak{a}\) in \(A\), i.e. the set of elements of \(A\) a power of which lies in \(\mathfrak{a}\).

The following results are valid if one assumes that \(A\) is noetherian and \(M\) of finite type.

Proposition 1.1

  1. \(\operatorname{Ass} M\) is a finite set.

  2. For an element of \(A\) to annihilate a non-zero element of \(M\), it is necessary and sufficient that it belong to one of the ideals associated to \(M\).

  3. The radical of the annihilator of \(M\), \(\mathfrak{r}(\operatorname{Ann} M)\), is the intersection of the ideals associated to \(M\) that are minimal (for the inclusion relation in \(\operatorname{Ass} M\)).

Proposition 1.2

Let \(\mathfrak{p} \) be a prime ideal of \(A\); the following assertions are equivalent:

  1. \(\mathfrak{p} \in \operatorname{Supp} M\).

  2. There exists \(\mathfrak{q} \in \operatorname{Ass} M\) such that \(\mathfrak{q} \subset\mathfrak{p} \).

  3. \(\mathfrak{p} \supset \operatorname{Ann} M\).

  4. (iii bis)

    \(\mathfrak{p} \supset \mathfrak{r}(\operatorname{Ann} M)\).

Proposition 1.3

Let \(N\) be an \(A\)-module of finite type; one has the formula:

\[ \operatorname{Ass} \operatorname{Hom}_A(N, M) = \operatorname{Supp} N \cap \operatorname{Ass} M. \]

2 Depth

Throughout this section, \(A\) denotes a commutative ring, \(I\) an ideal of \(A\), \(M\) and \(N\) two \(A\)-modules. We shall write \(X\) for the prime spectrum of \(A\) (we shall not make use of its structure sheaf in this section) and \(Y\) for the variety of \(I\), \(Y = \operatorname{Supp}(A/I) = \{\mathfrak{p} \in X, \ \mathfrak{p} \supset I\}\).

Lemma 2.1

Suppose that \(A\) is noetherian and that the modules \(M\) and \(N\) are of finite type. Suppose further that \(\operatorname{Supp} N=Y\). Then the following assertions are equivalent:

  1. \(\operatorname{Hom}_A(N, M)=0\).

  2. \(\operatorname{Supp} N \cap \operatorname{Ass} M = \emptyset\).

  3. The ideal \(I\) is not a divisor of \(0\) in \(M\), which means that for every \(m\in M\), \(Im=0\) implies \(m=0\).

  4. There exists in \(I\) an \(M\)-regular element. (An element \(a\) of \(A\) is said to be \(M\)-regular if the homothety of ratio \(a\) in \(M\) is injective.)

  5. For every \(\mathfrak{p} \in Y\), the maximal ideal \(\mathfrak{m}_\mathfrak{p}\) of the local ring \(A_\mathfrak{p}\) is not associated to \(M_\mathfrak{p}\). In formula: \(\mathfrak{p} A_\mathfrak{p} \notin \operatorname{Ass} M_\mathfrak{p}\).

Proof▾

(i) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) (ii) because \(\operatorname{Ass} \operatorname{Hom}_A(N, M)=\emptyset\) is equivalent to (ii) by proposition 1.3 and to (i) by an easy consequence of proposition 1.2.

(iii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (ii) by contradiction: "there exists \(\mathfrak{p} \in \operatorname{Supp} N \cap \operatorname{Ass} M\)" implies that \(\mathfrak{p} \supset I\) and that there exists \(m\in M\) whose annihilator is \(\mathfrak{p} \), hence \(Im \subset \mathfrak{p} m=0\), which contradicts (iii).

(iv) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (iii) trivially.

(ii) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) (iv) because \(\operatorname{Supp} N = Y\), hence (ii) means that \(I\) is contained in no ideal associated with \(M\) or again, (since the ideals associated with \(M\) are prime and finite in number), that \(I\) is not contained in the union of the ideals associated with \(M\). Now, by Prop. 1.1 (ii), this set is the set of elements of \(A\) which are not \(M\)-regular.

(i) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (v); indeed, if \(\operatorname{Hom}_A(N, M)=0\) and if \(\mathfrak{p} \in Y\), we deduce, by virtue of the formula

\[ \bigl(\operatorname{Hom}_A(N, M) \bigr)_\mathfrak{p} = \operatorname{Hom}_{A_\mathfrak{p}} \bigl(N_\mathfrak{p}, M_\mathfrak{p} \bigr), \]

that \(\operatorname{Hom}_{A_\mathfrak{p}} \bigl(N_\mathfrak{p}, M_\mathfrak{p} \bigr)=0\), hence, thanks to proposition 1.3,

\[ \operatorname{Supp} N_\mathfrak{p} \cap \operatorname{Ass} M_\mathfrak{p} = \emptyset, \]

now \(\mathfrak{p} A_\mathfrak{p} \in \operatorname{Supp} N_\mathfrak{p}, \) hence \( \mathfrak{p} A_\mathfrak{p} \notin \operatorname{Ass} M_\mathfrak{p}\).

(v) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (i); indeed, if \(\mathfrak{p} \in \operatorname{Ass} M\), there exists \(m\in M\) whose annihilator is \(\mathfrak{p}\), hence the canonical image of \(m\) in \(M_\mathfrak{p}\) is nonzero, hence its annihilator is an ideal which contains \(\mathfrak{p}\), hence \(\mathfrak{p} A_\mathfrak{p}\), hence is equal to it. The ideal \(\mathfrak{p} A_\mathfrak{p}\) is therefore associated with \(M_\mathfrak{p}\), hence \(\mathfrak{p} \notin Y\) by (v), whence (i).

Q.E.D.

We shall work on these conditions by replacing the functor \(\operatorname{Hom}\) by its derived functors.

Theorem 2.2

Let \(A\) be a commutative ring, \(I\) an ideal of \(A\), \(M\) an \(A\)-module. Let \(n\) be an integer.

  1. If there exists a sequence \(f_1, \ldots, f_{n+1}\), of elements of \(I\) which forms an \(M\)-regular sequence (i.e. if \(f_1\) is \(M\)-regular and if \(f_{i+1}\) is regular in \(M/(f_1, \ldots, f_i)M\) for \(i\leqslant n\)), then for every \(A\)-module \(N\) annihilated by a power of \(I\), we have:

    \[ \operatorname{Ext}_A^i(N, M)=0 \text{ for } i\leqslant n. \]

  2. If moreover \(A\) is noetherian, if \(M\) is of finite type, and if there exists an \(A\)-module \(N\) of finite type such that \(\operatorname{Supp} N = \operatorname{V}(I)\) and such that \(\operatorname{Ext}_A^i(N, M)=0\) for \(i\leqslant n\), then there exists a sequence \(f_1, \ldots, f_{n+1}\), of elements of \(I\) which is \(M\)-regular.

Let us first prove a), by induction. If \(n<0\) the statement is empty.

If \(n\geqslant 0\), suppose that a) is proved for \(n'<n\); by hypothesis there exists \(f_1\in I\) which is \(M\)-regular. Let us denote by \(f_1^i\) the multiplication by \(f_1\) in \(\operatorname{Ext}_A^i(N, M)\) and by \(f_1^M\) the multiplication by \(f_1\) in \(M\). The sequence

\[ \tag{2.1} 0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M \xrightarrow{f_1^M} M \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M/f_1M \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0 \]

is exact, hence so is the sequence:

\[ \operatorname{Ext}_A^{i-1} (N, M/f_1M) \xrightarrow{\delta}\operatorname{Ext}_A^i (N, M) \xrightarrow{f_1^i} \operatorname{Ext}_A^i (N, M). \]

By hypothesis \(I^rN=0\), hence \(f_1^0\) is nilpotent; \(\operatorname{Ext}^i\) is a universal functor, the same holds for \(f_1^i\) for every \(i\). Moreover, there exists a regular sequence in \(M/f_1 M\) which has \(n\) elements, hence, by the induction hypothesis,

\[ Ext_A^{i-1}(N, M)=0 \text{ if } i\leqslant n-1. \]

We deduce from this that if \(i\leqslant n\), \(f_1^i\) is at once nilpotent and injective hence \(\operatorname{Ext}_A^i(N, M)=0\).

Let us prove b), also by induction. If \(n<0\), the statement is empty.

If \(n=0\), b) follows from assertion (i) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (iv) of lemma 2.1.

If \(n>0\), by b) for \(n=0\), there exists an element \(f_1\in I\) which is \(M\)-regular; from the exact sequence (2.1), we deduce the exact sequence:

\[ \tag{2.2} \operatorname{Ext}_A^{i-1} (N, M) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Ext}_A^{i-1} (N, M/f_1M) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Ext}_A^i (N, M). \]

We conclude from this that the hypotheses of b) are satisfied for the module \(M/f_1M\) and for the integer \(n-1\). By the induction hypothesis, there exists a sequence of \(n\) elements of \(I\) which is regular for \(M/f_1M\), which implies that there exists a sequence of \(n+1\) elements of \(I\), beginning with \(f_1\), and which is \(M\)-regular.

This theorem invites us to generalize in the following manner the classical definition of the depth of a module of finite type over a noetherian ring:

Definition 2.3

Let \(A\) be a commutative ring with unit element, let \(M\) be an \(A\)-module, let \(I\) be an ideal of \(A\). We call \(I\)-depth of \(M\), and denote by \(\operatorname{prof}_I M\), the supremum of the set of natural integers \(n\), which are such that for every \(A\)-module of finite type \(N\) annihilated by a power of \(I\), one has

\[ \operatorname{Ext}_A^i(N, M)=0 \text{ for all } i< n. \]

We deduce from the preceding theorem that if \(n\) is the supremum of the lengths of the \(M\)-regular sequences of elements of \(I\), one has \(n\leqslant\operatorname{prof}_I M\).

More precisely:

Proposition 2.4

Let \(A\) be a commutative ring, \(I\) an ideal of \(A\) and let \(M\) be an \(A\)-module, let finally \(n\in \mathbf{N}\). Consider the assertions:

  1. \(n\leqslant \operatorname{prof}_I M\).

  2. For every \(A\)-module of finite type \(N\) which is annihilated by a power of \(I\), one has:

    \[ \operatorname{Ext}_A^i(N, M)=0 \text{ for } i<n. \]

  3. There exists an \(A\)-module of finite type \(N\) such that \(\operatorname{Supp} N = \operatorname{V}(I)\) and such that \(\operatorname{Ext}_A^i(N, M)=0\) if \(i<n\).

  4. There exists an \(M\)-regular sequence of length \(n\) consisting of elements of \(I\).

One has the following logical implications:

\[ \begin{array}{c}\displaystyle \textup{(1)} \mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow} \textup{(2)} \mathchoice{\Longleftarrow}{\Leftarrow}{\Leftarrow}{\Leftarrow} (4) \\ \big\Downarrow \\ \textup{(3)} \\ \end{array} \]

Moreover if \(A\) is noetherian and \(M\) of finite type, these conditions are equivalent.

Proof▾

(1) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) (2) by definition and (2) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (3) by taking \(N=A/I\); Moreover (4) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (2) by theorem 2.2 a). Finally, if \(A\) is noetherian and \(M\) of finite type, (3) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (4) by theorem 2.2 b).

Q.E.D.

We assume \(A\) noetherian and \(M\) of finite type until the end of this section.

Corollary 2.5

Let \(f\in I\) be an \(M\)-regular element, one has:

\[ \operatorname{prof}_I M = \operatorname{prof}_I (M/fM) + 1. \]

Indeed, if \(n\leqslant \operatorname{prof}_I (M/fM)\), there exists a sequence of elements of \(I\), \(f_1, \ldots, f_n\), which is \((M/fM)\)-regular; hence the sequence \(f, f_1, \ldots, f_n\) is \(M\)-regular, hence \(n+1 \leqslant \operatorname{prof}_I M\), hence \(\operatorname{prof}_I M\geqslant \operatorname{prof}_I (M/fM) + 1\). Moreover, by the exact sequence (2.2), if \(i \leqslant \operatorname{prof}_I M\), one has \(\operatorname{Ext}_A^{i-1} (N, M/fM)=0\), hence \(\operatorname{prof}_I M - 1 \leqslant \operatorname{prof}_I (M/fM)\).

1.2

Corollary 2.6

Every finite \(M\)-regular sequence, consisting of elements of \(I\), can be extended into a maximal \(M\)-regular sequence, whose length is necessarily equal to the \(I\)-depth of \(M\).

Remark 2.7

One can scarcely refrain from saying that an \(A\)-module is all the more beautiful the greater its depth. A module whose support does not meet \(\operatorname{V} (I)\) is among the most beautiful; indeed, one can prove that in order for \(\operatorname{prof}_I M\) to be finite, it is necessary and sufficient that \(\operatorname{Supp} M \cap \operatorname{V}(I) \neq \emptyset\).

Remark 2.8

If \(A\) is a semi-local ring, let \(\mathfrak{r}(A)\) be its radical and \(k=A/\mathfrak{r}(A)\) its residue ring. The interesting notion of depth is obtained by taking for \(I\) the radical of \(A\). We shall therefore agree to write simply \(\operatorname{prof} M\) for the \(\mathfrak{r}(A)\)-depth of an \(A\)-module \(M\). One recovers in this case the notion of "homological codimension", (cf. SERRE, op. cit. note (1), page ), which was denoted \(\operatorname{codh}_A M\), and which is defined as the infimum of the integers \(i\) such that \(\operatorname{Ext}_A^i(k, M)\neq 0\); indeed \(\operatorname{Supp} k = \operatorname{V}(\mathfrak{r}(A))\).

Proposition 2.9

If \(A\) is noetherian and \(M\) of finite type, one has:

\[ \operatorname{prof}_I M = \inf_{\mathfrak{p} \in \operatorname{V} (I)} \operatorname{prof} M_\mathfrak{p}. \]
Corollary 2.10

If \(A\) is a noetherian semi-local ring, and if \(M\) is an \(A\)-module of finite type, one has:

\[ \operatorname{prof} M = \inf_\mathfrak{m} \operatorname{prof} M_\mathfrak{m}, \]

where \(\mathfrak{m} \) ranges over the set of maximal ideals of \(A\).

The corollary follows immediately from proposition 2.9; indeed the prime ideals which contain the radical are the maximal ideals.

Moreover, let \(f\in I\); if \(f\) is \(M\)-regular, if \(\mathfrak{p}\in X\) and if \(\mathfrak{p}\supset I\), the image \(g\) of \(f\) in \(A_\mathfrak{p}\) belongs to \(\mathfrak{m}_\mathfrak{p}\), the maximal ideal of \(A_\mathfrak{p}\); moreover \(g\) is \(M_\mathfrak{p}\)-regular, as follows from the exact sequence

\[ \tag{2.3} 0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M_\mathfrak{p} \xrightarrow{g'} M_\mathfrak{p} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} (M/fM)_\mathfrak{p} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0, \]

where \(g'\) denotes the homothety with ratio \(g\) in \(M_\mathfrak{p}\). This exact sequence also gives that \((M/fM)_\mathfrak{p}\) is isomorphic to \(M_\mathfrak{p}/gM_\mathfrak{p}\); applying corollary 2.5 to \(M\) and to \(M_\mathfrak{p}\), we deduce, by induction, that \(\operatorname{prof}_I M\leqslant \nu(M)\), where one has set for every \(M\):

\[ \nu(M)=\inf_{\mathfrak{p}\in\operatorname{V}(I)} \operatorname{prof} M_\mathfrak{p}. \]

More precisely, again by induction, one knows, if \(f\) is \(M\)-regular, that \(\nu(M)= \nu(M/fM)+1\); it remains therefore to prove that if \(\nu(M)\geqslant 1\), there exists an \(M\)-regular element in \(I\). Now by applying (2.1) to \(M_\mathfrak{p}\), \(A_\mathfrak{p}\) and \(\mathfrak{m}_\mathfrak{p}\) for every \(\mathfrak{p}\in \operatorname{V}(I)\), one sees that \(\mathfrak{m}_\mathfrak{p} \notin \operatorname{Ass} M_\mathfrak{p}\), hence, by applying (2.1) to \(A\), \(M\) and \(I\), one has the conclusion.

Proposition 2.11

Let \(u:A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a homomorphism of noetherian rings. Let \(I\) be an ideal of \(A\), \(M\) an \(A\)-module of finite type. Set \(I_B=I\otimes_A B\) and \(M_B=M\otimes_A B\). If \(B\) is \(A\)-flat, one has:

\[ \operatorname{prof}_{I_B} M_B \geqslant \operatorname{prof}_I M; \]

moreover if \(B\) is faithfully flat over \(A\), one has equality.

Indeed, let \(N=A/I\); by flatness one has: \(N\otimes_A B=B/I_B\); set \(N_B=N\otimes_A B\). Again by flatness and noetherian hypotheses, one has:

\[ \operatorname{Ext}_B^i (N_B, M_B) = \operatorname{Ext}_A^i(N, M)\otimes_A B, \]

hence \(\operatorname{Ext}_A^i(N, M) = 0 \) implies \(\operatorname{Ext}_B^i(N_B, M_B)=0\), and the converse is true if \(B\) is faithfully flat over \(A\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.1

Suppose that \(A\) is noetherian and that the modules \(M\) and \(N\) are of finite type. Suppose further that \(\operatorname{Supp} N=Y\). Then the following assertions are equivalent:

  1. \(\operatorname{Hom}_A(N, M)=0\).

  2. \(\operatorname{Supp} N \cap \operatorname{Ass} M = \emptyset\).

  3. The ideal \(I\) is not a divisor of \(0\) in \(M\), which means that for every \(m\in M\), \(Im=0\) implies \(m=0\).

  4. There exists in \(I\) an \(M\)-regular element. (An element \(a\) of \(A\) is said to be \(M\)-regular if the homothety of ratio \(a\) in \(M\) is injective.)

  5. For every \(\mathfrak{p} \in Y\), the maximal ideal \(\mathfrak{m}_\mathfrak{p}\) of the local ring \(A_\mathfrak{p}\) is not associated to \(M_\mathfrak{p}\). In formula: \(\mathfrak{p} A_\mathfrak{p} \notin \operatorname{Ass} M_\mathfrak{p}\).

blocks.title: Proof
blocks.html:
Proof▾

(i) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) (ii) because \(\operatorname{Ass} \operatorname{Hom}_A(N, M)=\emptyset\) is equivalent to (ii) by proposition 1.3 and to (i) by an easy consequence of proposition 1.2.

(iii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (ii) by contradiction: "there exists \(\mathfrak{p} \in \operatorname{Supp} N \cap \operatorname{Ass} M\)" implies that \(\mathfrak{p} \supset I\) and that there exists \(m\in M\) whose annihilator is \(\mathfrak{p} \), hence \(Im \subset \mathfrak{p} m=0\), which contradicts (iii).

(iv) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (iii) trivially.

(ii) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) (iv) because \(\operatorname{Supp} N = Y\), hence (ii) means that \(I\) is contained in no ideal associated with \(M\) or again, (since the ideals associated with \(M\) are prime and finite in number), that \(I\) is not contained in the union of the ideals associated with \(M\). Now, by Prop. 1.1 (ii), this set is the set of elements of \(A\) which are not \(M\)-regular.

(i) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (v); indeed, if \(\operatorname{Hom}_A(N, M)=0\) and if \(\mathfrak{p} \in Y\), we deduce, by virtue of the formula

\[ \bigl(\operatorname{Hom}_A(N, M) \bigr)_\mathfrak{p} = \operatorname{Hom}_{A_\mathfrak{p}} \bigl(N_\mathfrak{p}, M_\mathfrak{p} \bigr), \]

that \(\operatorname{Hom}_{A_\mathfrak{p}} \bigl(N_\mathfrak{p}, M_\mathfrak{p} \bigr)=0\), hence, thanks to proposition 1.3,

\[ \operatorname{Supp} N_\mathfrak{p} \cap \operatorname{Ass} M_\mathfrak{p} = \emptyset, \]

now \(\mathfrak{p} A_\mathfrak{p} \in \operatorname{Supp} N_\mathfrak{p}, \) hence \( \mathfrak{p} A_\mathfrak{p} \notin \operatorname{Ass} M_\mathfrak{p}\).

(v) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (i); indeed, if \(\mathfrak{p} \in \operatorname{Ass} M\), there exists \(m\in M\) whose annihilator is \(\mathfrak{p}\), hence the canonical image of \(m\) in \(M_\mathfrak{p}\) is nonzero, hence its annihilator is an ideal which contains \(\mathfrak{p}\), hence \(\mathfrak{p} A_\mathfrak{p}\), hence is equal to it. The ideal \(\mathfrak{p} A_\mathfrak{p}\) is therefore associated with \(M_\mathfrak{p}\), hence \(\mathfrak{p} \notin Y\) by (v), whence (i).

Q.E.D.
We shall work on these conditions by replacing the functor \(\operatorname{Hom}\) by its derived functors.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.2

Let \(A\) be a commutative ring, \(I\) an ideal of \(A\), \(M\) an \(A\)-module. Let \(n\) be an integer.

  1. If there exists a sequence \(f_1, \ldots, f_{n+1}\), of elements of \(I\) which forms an \(M\)-regular sequence (i.e. if \(f_1\) is \(M\)-regular and if \(f_{i+1}\) is regular in \(M/(f_1, \ldots, f_i)M\) for \(i\leqslant n\)), then for every \(A\)-module \(N\) annihilated by a power of \(I\), we have:

    \[ \operatorname{Ext}_A^i(N, M)=0 \text{ for } i\leqslant n. \]

  2. If moreover \(A\) is noetherian, if \(M\) is of finite type, and if there exists an \(A\)-module \(N\) of finite type such that \(\operatorname{Supp} N = \operatorname{V}(I)\) and such that \(\operatorname{Ext}_A^i(N, M)=0\) for \(i\leqslant n\), then there exists a sequence \(f_1, \ldots, f_{n+1}\), of elements of \(I\) which is \(M\)-regular.
Let us first prove a), by induction. If \(n<0\) the statement is empty.

If \(n\geqslant 0\), suppose that a) is proved for \(n'<n\); by hypothesis there exists \(f_1\in I\) which is \(M\)-regular. Let us denote by \(f_1^i\) the multiplication by \(f_1\) in \(\operatorname{Ext}_A^i(N, M)\) and by \(f_1^M\) the multiplication by \(f_1\) in \(M\). The sequence

\[ \tag{2.1} 0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M \xrightarrow{f_1^M} M \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M/f_1M \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0 \]
is exact, hence so is the sequence:
\[ \operatorname{Ext}_A^{i-1} (N, M/f_1M) \xrightarrow{\delta}\operatorname{Ext}_A^i (N, M) \xrightarrow{f_1^i} \operatorname{Ext}_A^i (N, M). \]

By hypothesis \(I^rN=0\), hence \(f_1^0\) is nilpotent; \(\operatorname{Ext}^i\) is a universal functor, the same holds for \(f_1^i\) for every \(i\). Moreover, there exists a regular sequence in \(M/f_1 M\) which has \(n\) elements, hence, by the induction hypothesis,
\[ Ext_A^{i-1}(N, M)=0 \text{ if } i\leqslant n-1. \]
We deduce from this that if \(i\leqslant n\), \(f_1^i\) is at once nilpotent and injective hence \(\operatorname{Ext}_A^i(N, M)=0\).
Let us prove b), also by induction. If \(n<0\), the statement is empty.
If \(n=0\), b) follows from assertion (i) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (iv) of lemma 2.1.
If \(n>0\), by b) for \(n=0\), there exists an element \(f_1\in I\) which is \(M\)-regular; from the exact sequence (2.1), we deduce the exact sequence:

\[ \tag{2.2} \operatorname{Ext}_A^{i-1} (N, M) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Ext}_A^{i-1} (N, M/f_1M) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Ext}_A^i (N, M). \]

We conclude from this that the hypotheses of b) are satisfied for the module \(M/f_1M\) and for the integer \(n-1\). By the induction hypothesis, there exists a sequence of \(n\) elements of \(I\) which is regular for \(M/f_1M\), which implies that there exists a sequence of \(n+1\) elements of \(I\), beginning with \(f_1\), and which is \(M\)-regular.
This theorem invites us to generalize in the following manner the classical definition of the depth of a module of finite type over a noetherian ring:

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.3

Let \(A\) be a commutative ring with unit element, let \(M\) be an \(A\)-module, let \(I\) be an ideal of \(A\). We call \(I\)-depth of \(M\), and denote by \(\operatorname{prof}_I M\), the supremum of the set of natural integers \(n\), which are such that for every \(A\)-module of finite type \(N\) annihilated by a power of \(I\), one has

\[ \operatorname{Ext}_A^i(N, M)=0 \text{ for all } i< n. \]
We deduce from the preceding theorem that if \(n\) is the supremum of the lengths of the \(M\)-regular sequences of elements of \(I\), one has \(n\leqslant\operatorname{prof}_I M\).
More precisely:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.4

Let \(A\) be a commutative ring, \(I\) an ideal of \(A\) and let \(M\) be an \(A\)-module, let finally \(n\in \mathbf{N}\). Consider the assertions:

  1. \(n\leqslant \operatorname{prof}_I M\).

  2. For every \(A\)-module of finite type \(N\) which is annihilated by a power of \(I\), one has:

    \[ \operatorname{Ext}_A^i(N, M)=0 \text{ for } i<n. \]

  3. There exists an \(A\)-module of finite type \(N\) such that \(\operatorname{Supp} N = \operatorname{V}(I)\) and such that \(\operatorname{Ext}_A^i(N, M)=0\) if \(i<n\).

  4. There exists an \(M\)-regular sequence of length \(n\) consisting of elements of \(I\).

One has the following logical implications:

\[ \begin{array}{c}\displaystyle \textup{(1)} \mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow} \textup{(2)} \mathchoice{\Longleftarrow}{\Leftarrow}{\Leftarrow}{\Leftarrow} (4) \\ \big\Downarrow \\ \textup{(3)} \\ \end{array} \]

Moreover if \(A\) is noetherian and \(M\) of finite type, these conditions are equivalent.

blocks.title: Proof
blocks.html:
Proof▾

(1) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) (2) by definition and (2) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (3) by taking \(N=A/I\); Moreover (4) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (2) by theorem 2.2 a). Finally, if \(A\) is noetherian and \(M\) of finite type, (3) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (4) by theorem 2.2 b).

Q.E.D.
We assume \(A\) noetherian and \(M\) of finite type until the end of this section.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.5

Let \(f\in I\) be an \(M\)-regular element, one has:

\[ \operatorname{prof}_I M = \operatorname{prof}_I (M/fM) + 1. \]

Indeed, if \(n\leqslant \operatorname{prof}_I (M/fM)\), there exists a sequence of elements of \(I\), \(f_1, \ldots, f_n\), which is \((M/fM)\)-regular; hence the sequence \(f, f_1, \ldots, f_n\) is \(M\)-regular, hence \(n+1 \leqslant \operatorname{prof}_I M\), hence \(\operatorname{prof}_I M\geqslant \operatorname{prof}_I (M/fM) + 1\). Moreover, by the exact sequence (2.2), if \(i \leqslant \operatorname{prof}_I M\), one has \(\operatorname{Ext}_A^{i-1} (N, M/fM)=0\), hence \(\operatorname{prof}_I M - 1 \leqslant \operatorname{prof}_I (M/fM)\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.6

Every finite \(M\)-regular sequence, consisting of elements of \(I\), can be extended into a maximal \(M\)-regular sequence, whose length is necessarily equal to the \(I\)-depth of \(M\).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 2.7

One can scarcely refrain from saying that an \(A\)-module is all the more beautiful the greater its depth. A module whose support does not meet \(\operatorname{V} (I)\) is among the most beautiful; indeed, one can prove that in order for \(\operatorname{prof}_I M\) to be finite, it is necessary and sufficient that \(\operatorname{Supp} M \cap \operatorname{V}(I) \neq \emptyset\).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 2.8

If \(A\) is a semi-local ring, let \(\mathfrak{r}(A)\) be its radical and \(k=A/\mathfrak{r}(A)\) its residue ring. The interesting notion of depth is obtained by taking for \(I\) the radical of \(A\). We shall therefore agree to write simply \(\operatorname{prof} M\) for the \(\mathfrak{r}(A)\)-depth of an \(A\)-module \(M\). One recovers in this case the notion of "homological codimension", (cf. SERRE, op. cit. note (1), page ), which was denoted \(\operatorname{codh}_A M\), and which is defined as the infimum of the integers \(i\) such that \(\operatorname{Ext}_A^i(k, M)\neq 0\); indeed \(\operatorname{Supp} k = \operatorname{V}(\mathfrak{r}(A))\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.9

If \(A\) is noetherian and \(M\) of finite type, one has:

\[ \operatorname{prof}_I M = \inf_{\mathfrak{p} \in \operatorname{V} (I)} \operatorname{prof} M_\mathfrak{p}. \]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.10

If \(A\) is a noetherian semi-local ring, and if \(M\) is an \(A\)-module of finite type, one has:

\[ \operatorname{prof} M = \inf_\mathfrak{m} \operatorname{prof} M_\mathfrak{m}, \]

where \(\mathfrak{m} \) ranges over the set of maximal ideals of \(A\).
The corollary follows immediately from proposition 2.9; indeed the prime ideals which contain the radical are the maximal ideals.

Moreover, let \(f\in I\); if \(f\) is \(M\)-regular, if \(\mathfrak{p}\in X\) and if \(\mathfrak{p}\supset I\), the image \(g\) of \(f\) in \(A_\mathfrak{p}\) belongs to \(\mathfrak{m}_\mathfrak{p}\), the maximal ideal of \(A_\mathfrak{p}\); moreover \(g\) is \(M_\mathfrak{p}\)-regular, as follows from the exact sequence

\[ \tag{2.3} 0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M_\mathfrak{p} \xrightarrow{g'} M_\mathfrak{p} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} (M/fM)_\mathfrak{p} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0, \]

where \(g'\) denotes the homothety with ratio \(g\) in \(M_\mathfrak{p}\). This exact sequence also gives that \((M/fM)_\mathfrak{p}\) is isomorphic to \(M_\mathfrak{p}/gM_\mathfrak{p}\); applying corollary 2.5 to \(M\) and to \(M_\mathfrak{p}\), we deduce, by induction, that \(\operatorname{prof}_I M\leqslant \nu(M)\), where one has set for every \(M\):
\[ \nu(M)=\inf_{\mathfrak{p}\in\operatorname{V}(I)} \operatorname{prof} M_\mathfrak{p}. \]

More precisely, again by induction, one knows, if \(f\) is \(M\)-regular, that \( u(M)= u(M/fM)+1\); it remains therefore to prove that if \( u(M)\geqslant 1\), there exists an \(M\)-regular element in \(I\). Now by applying (2.1) to \(M_\mathfrak{p}\), \(A_\mathfrak{p}\) and \(\mathfrak{m}_\mathfrak{p}\) for every \(\mathfrak{p}\in \operatorname{V}(I)\), one sees that \(\mathfrak{m}_\mathfrak{p} otin \operatorname{Ass} M_\mathfrak{p}\), hence, by applying (2.1) to \(A\), \(M\) and \(I\), one has the conclusion.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.11

Let \(u:A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a homomorphism of noetherian rings. Let \(I\) be an ideal of \(A\), \(M\) an \(A\)-module of finite type. Set \(I_B=I\otimes_A B\) and \(M_B=M\otimes_A B\). If \(B\) is \(A\)-flat, one has:

\[ \operatorname{prof}_{I_B} M_B \geqslant \operatorname{prof}_I M; \]

moreover if \(B\) is faithfully flat over \(A\), one has equality.
Indeed, let \(N=A/I\); by flatness one has: \(N\otimes_A B=B/I_B\); set \(N_B=N\otimes_A B\). Again by flatness and noetherian hypotheses, one has:
\[ \operatorname{Ext}_B^i (N_B, M_B) = \operatorname{Ext}_A^i(N, M)\otimes_A B, \]
hence \(\operatorname{Ext}_A^i(N, M) = 0 \) implies \(\operatorname{Ext}_B^i(N_B, M_B)=0\), and the converse is true if \(B\) is faithfully flat over \(A\).

pages.title: Depth and topological properties
pages.html:
3 Depth and topological properties

Lemma 3.1

Let \(X\) be a topological space, \(Y\) a closed subspace, and let \(F\) be a sheaf of abelian groups on \(X\). Put \(U=X- Y\). If \(n\) is an integer, the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y^i (X, F)=0\) if \(i<n\).

  2. For every open set \(V\) of \(X\), the group homomorphism

    \[ \operatorname{H}^i(V, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^i(V\cap U, F) \]

    is bijective if \(i<n-1\) and injective if \(i=n-1\).

  3. For every open set \(V\) of \(X\),

    \[ \operatorname{H}_{Y\cap V}^i(V, F_{\mid V})=0 \text{ if } i<n. \]
Proof▾

(ii) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) (iii); indeed, let \(V\) be an open set of \(X\), put \(X'=V\), \(Y'=Y\cap V\), \(F'=F_{\mid V}\), \(U'=X'- Y'\); \(Y'\) is closed in \(X'\), so we have an exact sequence:

\[ \operatorname{H}_{Y'}^i(X', F') \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^i(X', F') \xrightarrow{\rho_i} \operatorname{H}^i(U', F') \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_{Y'}^{i+1}(X', F'). \]

If the extreme terms are zero, the homomorphism \(\rho_i\) is bijective, and if the left-hand term is zero, \(\rho_i\) is injective. Hence (iii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (ii). Conversely, if \(i<n\), \(\operatorname{H}_{Y'}^i(X', F')\) is zero since \(\rho_i\) is injective and \(\rho_{i-1}\) surjective.

(i) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (iii); indeed the "passage from local to global" spectral sequence gives:

\[ \operatorname{H}^p\bigl(X, \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y^q(X, F)\bigr) \mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_Y^*(X, F). \]

Now, by hypothesis \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y^q(X, F) =0\) if \(q<n\), hence \((\operatorname{H}_Y (X, F))^{p+q} = 0\) if \(p+q<n\).

(iii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (i); indeed (iii) expresses that the presheaf

\[ V \rightsquigarrow \operatorname{H}_{Y\cap V}^i(V, F_{\mid V}) \]

is zero, hence so is the associated sheaf, which is \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y^i (X, F)\), since \(Y\) is closed.

Q.E.D.
Remark 3.2

If \(n\geqslant 2\), one may omit the condition that \(\rho_{n-1}\) be injective, since the sheaf associated with the presheaf

\[ V \rightsquigarrow \operatorname{H}^{n-2}(V\cap U, F) \]

is isomorphic to \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y^{n-1} (X, F)\), but by hypothesis (ii) for \(i=n-2\), this presheaf is isomorphic to the presheaf \(V \rightsquigarrow \operatorname{H}^i(V, F)\) whose associated sheaf is zero.

Proposition 3.3

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme, \(Y\) a closed subprescheme of \(X\), \(F\) a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module. The conditions of Lemma 3.1 are equivalent to each of the following conditions:

  1. For every \(y\in Y\), we have \(\operatorname{prof} F_x \geqslant n\);

  2. For every coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module \(G\) on \(X\), with support contained in \(Y\), we have

    \[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}^i(G, F)=0 \text{ if } i<n; \]

  3. There exists a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module \(G\) whose support is equal to \(Y\) and such that

    \[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}^i(G, F)=0 \text{ if } i<n. \]

If \(X\) is affine, we have done everything needed to prove the equivalence of the three conditions of Proposition 3.3, now they are local, so it suffices to prove (i) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (vi) and (v) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (i).

Let \(J\) be the ideal of \(Y\), it is a coherent sheaf of ideals; let \(\mathscr{O}_{X_m} = \mathscr{O}_X/J^{m+1}\), it is a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module whose support is equal to \(Y\), and we know that

\[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y^i(X, F) = \varinjlim_m \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}^i(\mathscr{O}_{X_m}, F), \]

hence (v) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (i). Moreover, the transition morphisms are epimorphisms in the projective system of the \(\mathscr{O}_{X_m}\).

If the functor \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^i\) is left exact in its first argument, at least when the latter lies in the category of coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Modules with support contained in \(Y\), the transition morphisms of the inductive system obtained by applying \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^i\) to the \(\mathscr{O}_{X_m}\) will be injective, now (i) implies that the limit is zero, hence (i) will imply that the modules \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}^i(\mathscr{O}_{X_m}, F)\) are zero for every \(m\). Let us argue by induction. The statement is trivial for \(n<0\). Suppose that (i) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (vi) for \(n<q\), then (i) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (v), hence, by the exact sequence of the \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits\), \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^q\) is left exact in its first argument, hence the modules \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}^q(\mathscr{O}_{X_m}, F)\) are zero for every \(m\). Hence (i) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (vi) for \(n\leqslant q\). Q.E.D.

Example 3.4

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring, \(\mathfrak{m}\) its maximal ideal, \(M\) a finitely generated \(A\)-module, and finally let \(n\) be an integer. Put \(X=\operatorname{Spec} (A)\), \(Y=\{\mathfrak{m}\}\), \(U=X-Y\). Let \(F\) be the sheaf associated with \(M\). The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. \(\operatorname{prof} M \geqslant n\);

  2. the natural homomorphism

    \[ \operatorname{H}^i(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^i(U, F) \]

    injective if \(i=n-1\), is bijective if \(i<n-1\);

  3. \(\operatorname{Ext}_A^i(k, M) =0\) if \(i<n\), where \(k=A/\mathfrak{m}\);

  4. \(\operatorname{H}_Y^i(X, F)=0\) if \(i<n\).

Corollary 3.5

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme, \(Y\) a closed subprescheme of \(X\), \(F\) a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module; the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. for every \(x\in Y\), \(\operatorname{prof} F_x \geqslant 2\);

  2. for every open set \(V\) of \(X\), the natural homomorphism

    \[ \operatorname{H}^0(V, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(V\cap(X-Y), F) \]

    is bijective.

Theorem 3.6 (HARTSHORNE)

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme, \(Y\) a closed subprescheme of \(X\). Suppose that, for every \(x\in Y\), \(\operatorname{prof} \mathscr{O}_{X, x} \geqslant 2\); then the natural map

\[ \pi_0(X) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_0(X-Y) \]

is bijective.

Proof▾

Since \(X\) is locally noetherian, \(X\) is locally connected; it therefore suffices to prove that for \(X\) to be connected it is necessary and sufficient that \(X-Y\) be so. Now, for a space \((X, \mathscr{O}_X)\) ringed in local rings to be connected, it is necessary and sufficient that \(\operatorname{H}^0(X, \mathscr{O}_X)\) not be a direct composite of two nonzero rings. But the hypothesis implies, by Corollary 3.5 applied to \(F=\mathscr{O}_X\), that the homomorphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^0(X, \mathscr{O}_X) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(X-Y, \mathscr{O}_X) \]

is an isomorphism, whence the conclusion.

Q.E.D.
Corollary 3.7

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme. Let \(d\) be an integer such that \(\dim \mathscr{O}_{X, x} \geqslant d\) implies \(\operatorname{prof} \mathscr{O}_{X, x} \geqslant 2\). Then, if \(X\) is connected, \(X\) is connected in codimension \(d-1\), i.e. if \(X'\) and \(X''\) are two irreducible components of \(X\), there exists a sequence of irreducible components :

\[ X'=X_0, X_1, \ldots, X_n=X'' \]

such that for every \(i\), \(1\leqslant i \leqslant n\), the codimension of \(X_{i-1}\cap X_i\) in \(X\) is less than or equal to \(d-1\).

Let us first remark that if \(X\) is Cohen-Macaulay, \(d=2\) will enjoy the property mentioned above. In this connection, let us recall that the codimension of \(Y\) in \(X\) is defined as the infimum of the dimensions of the local rings in \(X\) of the points of \(Y\).

Proof▾

Let \(\mathscr{F}\) be the set of closed subsets of \(X\) whose codimension in \(X\) is greater than or equal to \(d\). One notes that \(\mathscr{F}\) is an antifilter of closed subsets of \(X\). Moreover, for a closed set \(Y\) of \(X\) to be an element of \(\mathscr{F}\), it is necessary and sufficient that, for every \(y\in Y\), there exist an open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(X\) such that \(Y\cap V\) be of codimension \(\geqslant d\) in \(V\). Finally, if \(X\) is connected and if \(Y\in \mathscr{F}\), \(X-Y\) is connected by Hartshorne's theorem. The corollary thus follows from the following lemma, which is of a purely topological nature.

Lemma 3.8

Let \(X\) be a connected and locally noetherian topological space, and let \(\mathscr{F}\) be an antifilter of closed subsets of \(X\). Suppose that every closed set \(Y\subset X\) that belongs locally to \(\mathscr{F}\), (i.e. for every point \(x\in X\) there exists an open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(x\) in \(X\) and a \(Y'\in \mathscr{F}\) such that \(V\cap Y=V\cap Y'\)), belongs to \(\mathscr{F}\). The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. for every \(Y\in \mathscr{F}\), \(X-Y\) is connected;

  2. if \(X'\) and \(X''\) are two distinct irreducible components of \(X\), there exists a sequence of irreducible components of \(X\), \(X_0, X_1, \ldots, X_n\), such that \(X'=X_0\), \(X''=X_n\) and, for every \(i\), \(1\leqslant i\leqslant n\), \(X_{i-1} \cap X_i \notin \mathscr{F}\).

(ii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (i). Let \(Y\in \mathscr{F}\), we must prove that the open set \(U=X-Y\) is connected. Now, if \(U'\) and \(U''\) are two irreducible components of \(U\), there exist two irreducible components \(X'\) and \(X''\) of \(X\) such that \(X''\cap U=U''\) and \(X'\cap U=U'\); let \(X_0, \ldots X_n\) be a sequence of irreducible components of \(X\) possessing the property mentioned above; if one puts \(U_i=X_i\cap U\), \(0\leqslant i\leqslant n\), the \(U_i\) will be irreducible components of \(U\), moreover \(U_{i}\cap U_{i+1}\) is nonempty if \(0\leqslant i\leqslant n\), for otherwise, \(X_{i}\cap X_{i+1} \subset Y\) would be \(\in\) \(\mathscr{F}\), which is contrary to the choice of the sequence of the \(X_i\). This implies that \(U\) is connected.

(i) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (ii). Let \(Y=\bigcup_{X', X''} X'\cap X''\) where one requires that \(X'\) and \(X''\) be two distinct irreducible components of \(X\) such that \(X'\cap X'' \in \mathscr{F}\). The family of the \(X'\cap X''\) is locally finite since \(X\) is locally noetherian, moreover the \(X'\cap X''\) are closed, hence \(Y\) is closed. Furthermore, \(Y\) belongs locally to \(\mathscr{F}\), hence \(Y\in \mathscr{F}\). Hence \(U=X-Y\) is connected. Let \(X'\) and \(X''\) be two distinct irreducible components of \(X\), let \(U'\) and \(U''\) be their traces on \(U\), which are nonempty by construction of \(Y\). These are irreducible components of \(U\), now \(U\) is connected, hence, \(U\) being locally noetherian, there exists a sequence of irreducible components \(U_0, \ldots, U_n\) of \(U\), such that \(U_0=U'\), \(U_n=U''\) and \(U_{i}\cap U_{i+1}\neq \emptyset\) and \(\neq U_{i}, 0\leqslant i < n\). Let \(X_0, \ldots, X_n\) be the sequence of irreducible components of \(X\) which is such that \(X_{i}\cap U=U_{i}\); if \(X_{i}\cap X_{i+1}\in \mathscr{F}\), by construction of \(\mathscr{F}\), \(U_{i}\cap U_{i+1}=\emptyset\) or \(U_{i}=U_{i+1}\) which is not possible by the choice of the \(U_{i}\).

Q.E.D.
Corollary 3.9

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring. Suppose that for every prime ideal \(\mathfrak{p}\) of \(A\), we have:

\[ (\dim A_\mathfrak{p} \geqslant 2) \mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow} (\operatorname{prof} A_\mathfrak{p} \geqslant 2) \]

Suppose moreover that \(A\) satisfies the chain condition1. Then, for every \(\mathfrak{p}\), minimal prime ideal of \(A\), \(\dim A/\mathfrak{p} =\dim A\), or again, all the irreducible components of \(\operatorname{Spec} A\) have the same dimension: that of \(A\).

If \(X'\) and \(X''\) are two irreducible components of \(X\), one joins them by a chain having the properties enumerated in 3.7; it then suffices to prove that two successive components have the same dimension, which follows from the second hypothesis.

Example 3.10

Let \(X\) be the union of two complementary vector subspaces of respective dimensions \(2\) and \(3\) in a vector space of dimension \(5\); more precisely, let \(X=\operatorname{Spec} A\), with \(A=B/{\mathfrak{p} \cap \mathfrak{q}}\), where \(B=k[X_1, \ldots, X_5]\), \(\mathfrak{p}\) is the ideal generated by \(X_1, X_2, X_3\) and \(\mathfrak{q}\) the ideal generated by \(X_4\) and \(X_5\); \(X\) can be disconnected by the point of intersection \(x\) of the two vector subspaces, hence the depth of \(\mathscr{O}_{X, x}\) is equal to \(1\), since it cannot be \(\geqslant 2\) by virtue of Theorem 3.6. Another reason: the equidimensionality conclusion of the preceding corollary fails.

More generally, taking a union \(X\) of two vector subspaces of dimension \(p, q\geqslant 2\) in a vector space of dimension \(p+q\), for no embedding of \(X\) into a regular scheme is \(X\) even set-theoretically a complete intersection at the origin, since (up to modifying it without changing the underlying topological space in a neighbourhood of the origin), \(X\) would be Cohen-Macaulay hence of depth \(\geqslant 2\) at the origin, which is not the case.

Remark 3.11

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme, \(Y\) a closed subprescheme of \(X\), \(F\) an \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module. Depth is a purely topological notion which is expressed in terms of the vanishing of the \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y^i(X, F)\) for \(i<n\). One also wishes to study these sheaves for a given \(i\), or for \(i>n\). In this connection one proves the following result:

Lemma 3.12

Let \(m\) be an integer; for \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y^i(X, F)=0\), \(i>m\) for every coherent \(F\), it is necessary and sufficient that this hold for \(F=\mathscr{O}_X\).

By inductive limit it is then true for every quasi-coherent sheaf. For example, if \(Y\) can be described locally by \(m\) equations, or, as one says, if \(Y\) is locally set-theoretically a complete intersection (which happens for example if \(X\) and \(Y\) are nonsingular) it follows from the computation of the \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y^i(X, F)\) by the Koszul complex that these sheaves are zero for \(i>m\). This fact has moreover been used implicitly in Example 3.10. This cohomological condition is however not sufficient, as the following example proves:

Example 3.13

Let \(X=\operatorname{Spec} (A)\), where \(A\) is a noetherian local ring, normal, of dimension \(2\). Let \(Y\) be a curve in \(X\). One can prove that the complement of the curve is an affine open set, hence \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y^i(X, \mathscr{O}_X) \approx \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y^i(X, \mathscr{O}_X)\) \(\operatorname{H}^{i-1}(X-Y, \mathscr{O}_X)=0\). However one can construct a curve which is not described by an equation.

We shall seek2 conditions for the \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y^i(X, F)\) to be coherent for a given \(i\), which is not the case in general, as obvious examples show, for instance \(\operatorname{H}_\mathfrak{m}^n(A)\) for \(A\) a noetherian local ring of dimension \(n>0\); when for example \(A\) is a discrete valuation ring with field of fractions \(K\), one finds \(\operatorname{H}_\mathfrak{m}^1(A) \simeq K/A\), which is not a finitely generated module over \(A\). To enlighten the reader, let us say that the problem posed is equivalent to the following: Let \(f:U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) be an open immersion, let \(G\) be a coherent sheaf on \(U\), find criteria for the higher direct images \(\operatorname{R}^i f_* G\) to be coherent sheaves on \(X\) for a given \(i\). These conditions are necessary for the use of formal geometry that we have seen in Exp IX and following.

blocks.html: 3 Depth and topological properties

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.1

Let \(X\) be a topological space, \(Y\) a closed subspace, and let \(F\) be a sheaf of abelian groups on \(X\). Put \(U=X- Y\). If \(n\) is an integer, the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y^i (X, F)=0\) if \(i<n\).

  2. For every open set \(V\) of \(X\), the group homomorphism

    \[ \operatorname{H}^i(V, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^i(V\cap U, F) \]

    is bijective if \(i<n-1\) and injective if \(i=n-1\).

  3. For every open set \(V\) of \(X\),

    \[ \operatorname{H}_{Y\cap V}^i(V, F_{\mid V})=0 \text{ if } i<n. \]

blocks.title: Proof
blocks.html:
Proof▾

(ii) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) (iii); indeed, let \(V\) be an open set of \(X\), put \(X'=V\), \(Y'=Y\cap V\), \(F'=F_{\mid V}\), \(U'=X'- Y'\); \(Y'\) is closed in \(X'\), so we have an exact sequence:

\[ \operatorname{H}_{Y'}^i(X', F') \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^i(X', F') \xrightarrow{\rho_i} \operatorname{H}^i(U', F') \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_{Y'}^{i+1}(X', F'). \]

If the extreme terms are zero, the homomorphism \(\rho_i\) is bijective, and if the left-hand term is zero, \(\rho_i\) is injective. Hence (iii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (ii). Conversely, if \(i<n\), \(\operatorname{H}_{Y'}^i(X', F')\) is zero since \(\rho_i\) is injective and \(\rho_{i-1}\) surjective.

(i) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (iii); indeed the "passage from local to global" spectral sequence gives:

\[ \operatorname{H}^p\bigl(X, \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y^q(X, F)\bigr) \mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_Y^*(X, F). \]

Now, by hypothesis \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y^q(X, F) =0\) if \(q<n\), hence \((\operatorname{H}_Y (X, F))^{p+q} = 0\) if \(p+q<n\).

(iii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (i); indeed (iii) expresses that the presheaf

\[ V \rightsquigarrow \operatorname{H}_{Y\cap V}^i(V, F_{\mid V}) \]

is zero, hence so is the associated sheaf, which is \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y^i (X, F)\), since \(Y\) is closed.

Q.E.D.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 3.2

If \(n\geqslant 2\), one may omit the condition that \(\rho_{n-1}\) be injective, since the sheaf associated with the presheaf

\[ V \rightsquigarrow \operatorname{H}^{n-2}(V\cap U, F) \]

is isomorphic to \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y^{n-1} (X, F)\), but by hypothesis (ii) for \(i=n-2\), this presheaf is isomorphic to the presheaf \(V \rightsquigarrow \operatorname{H}^i(V, F)\) whose associated sheaf is zero.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.3

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme, \(Y\) a closed subprescheme of \(X\), \(F\) a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module. The conditions of Lemma 3.1 are equivalent to each of the following conditions:

  1. For every \(y\in Y\), we have \(\operatorname{prof} F_x \geqslant n\);

  2. For every coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module \(G\) on \(X\), with support contained in \(Y\), we have

    \[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}^i(G, F)=0 \text{ if } i<n; \]

  3. There exists a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module \(G\) whose support is equal to \(Y\) and such that

    \[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}^i(G, F)=0 \text{ if } i<n. \]

If \(X\) is affine, we have done everything needed to prove the equivalence of the three conditions of Proposition 3.3, now they are local, so it suffices to prove (i) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (vi) and (v) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (i).

Let \(J\) be the ideal of \(Y\), it is a coherent sheaf of ideals; let \(\mathscr{O}_{X_m} = \mathscr{O}_X/J^{m+1}\), it is a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module whose support is equal to \(Y\), and we know that
\[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y^i(X, F) = \varinjlim_m \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}^i(\mathscr{O}_{X_m}, F), \]
hence (v) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (i). Moreover, the transition morphisms are epimorphisms in the projective system of the \(\mathscr{O}_{X_m}\).

If the functor \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}} olimits^i\) is left exact in its first argument, at least when the latter lies in the category of coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Modules with support contained in \(Y\), the transition morphisms of the inductive system obtained by applying \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}} olimits^i\) to the \(\mathscr{O}_{X_m}\) will be injective, now (i) implies that the limit is zero, hence (i) will imply that the modules \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}} olimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}^i(\mathscr{O}_{X_m}, F)\) are zero for every \(m\). Let us argue by induction. The statement is trivial for \(n<0\). Suppose that (i) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (vi) for \(n<q\), then (i) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (v), hence, by the exact sequence of the \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}} olimits\), \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}} olimits^q\) is left exact in its first argument, hence the modules \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}} olimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}^q(\mathscr{O}_{X_m}, F)\) are zero for every \(m\). Hence (i) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (vi) for \(n\leqslant q\). Q.E.D.

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example 3.4

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring, \(\mathfrak{m}\) its maximal ideal, \(M\) a finitely generated \(A\)-module, and finally let \(n\) be an integer. Put \(X=\operatorname{Spec} (A)\), \(Y=\{\mathfrak{m}\}\), \(U=X-Y\). Let \(F\) be the sheaf associated with \(M\). The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. \(\operatorname{prof} M \geqslant n\);

  2. the natural homomorphism

    \[ \operatorname{H}^i(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^i(U, F) \]

    injective if \(i=n-1\), is bijective if \(i<n-1\);

  3. \(\operatorname{Ext}_A^i(k, M) =0\) if \(i<n\), where \(k=A/\mathfrak{m}\);

  4. \(\operatorname{H}_Y^i(X, F)=0\) if \(i<n\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.5

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme, \(Y\) a closed subprescheme of \(X\), \(F\) a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module; the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. for every \(x\in Y\), \(\operatorname{prof} F_x \geqslant 2\);

  2. for every open set \(V\) of \(X\), the natural homomorphism

    \[ \operatorname{H}^0(V, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(V\cap(X-Y), F) \]

    is bijective.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.6 (HARTSHORNE)

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme, \(Y\) a closed subprescheme of \(X\). Suppose that, for every \(x\in Y\), \(\operatorname{prof} \mathscr{O}_{X, x} \geqslant 2\); then the natural map

\[ \pi_0(X) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_0(X-Y) \]

is bijective.

blocks.title: Proof
blocks.html:
Proof▾

Since \(X\) is locally noetherian, \(X\) is locally connected; it therefore suffices to prove that for \(X\) to be connected it is necessary and sufficient that \(X-Y\) be so. Now, for a space \((X, \mathscr{O}_X)\) ringed in local rings to be connected, it is necessary and sufficient that \(\operatorname{H}^0(X, \mathscr{O}_X)\) not be a direct composite of two nonzero rings. But the hypothesis implies, by Corollary 3.5 applied to \(F=\mathscr{O}_X\), that the homomorphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^0(X, \mathscr{O}_X) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(X-Y, \mathscr{O}_X) \]

is an isomorphism, whence the conclusion.

Q.E.D.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.7

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme. Let \(d\) be an integer such that \(\dim \mathscr{O}_{X, x} \geqslant d\) implies \(\operatorname{prof} \mathscr{O}_{X, x} \geqslant 2\). Then, if \(X\) is connected, \(X\) is connected in codimension \(d-1\), i.e. if \(X'\) and \(X''\) are two irreducible components of \(X\), there exists a sequence of irreducible components :

\[ X'=X_0, X_1, \ldots, X_n=X'' \]

such that for every \(i\), \(1\leqslant i \leqslant n\), the codimension of \(X_{i-1}\cap X_i\) in \(X\) is less than or equal to \(d-1\).

Let us first remark that if \(X\) is Cohen-Macaulay, \(d=2\) will enjoy the property mentioned above. In this connection, let us recall that the codimension of \(Y\) in \(X\) is defined as the infimum of the dimensions of the local rings in \(X\) of the points of \(Y\).

blocks.title: Proof
blocks.html:
Proof▾

Let \(\mathscr{F}\) be the set of closed subsets of \(X\) whose codimension in \(X\) is greater than or equal to \(d\). One notes that \(\mathscr{F}\) is an antifilter of closed subsets of \(X\). Moreover, for a closed set \(Y\) of \(X\) to be an element of \(\mathscr{F}\), it is necessary and sufficient that, for every \(y\in Y\), there exist an open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(X\) such that \(Y\cap V\) be of codimension \(\geqslant d\) in \(V\). Finally, if \(X\) is connected and if \(Y\in \mathscr{F}\), \(X-Y\) is connected by Hartshorne's theorem. The corollary thus follows from the following lemma, which is of a purely topological nature.

Lemma 3.8

Let \(X\) be a connected and locally noetherian topological space, and let \(\mathscr{F}\) be an antifilter of closed subsets of \(X\). Suppose that every closed set \(Y\subset X\) that belongs locally to \(\mathscr{F}\), (i.e. for every point \(x\in X\) there exists an open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(x\) in \(X\) and a \(Y'\in \mathscr{F}\) such that \(V\cap Y=V\cap Y'\)), belongs to \(\mathscr{F}\). The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. for every \(Y\in \mathscr{F}\), \(X-Y\) is connected;

  2. if \(X'\) and \(X''\) are two distinct irreducible components of \(X\), there exists a sequence of irreducible components of \(X\), \(X_0, X_1, \ldots, X_n\), such that \(X'=X_0\), \(X''=X_n\) and, for every \(i\), \(1\leqslant i\leqslant n\), \(X_{i-1} \cap X_i \notin \mathscr{F}\).

(ii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (i). Let \(Y\in \mathscr{F}\), we must prove that the open set \(U=X-Y\) is connected. Now, if \(U'\) and \(U''\) are two irreducible components of \(U\), there exist two irreducible components \(X'\) and \(X''\) of \(X\) such that \(X''\cap U=U''\) and \(X'\cap U=U'\); let \(X_0, \ldots X_n\) be a sequence of irreducible components of \(X\) possessing the property mentioned above; if one puts \(U_i=X_i\cap U\), \(0\leqslant i\leqslant n\), the \(U_i\) will be irreducible components of \(U\), moreover \(U_{i}\cap U_{i+1}\) is nonempty if \(0\leqslant i\leqslant n\), for otherwise, \(X_{i}\cap X_{i+1} \subset Y\) would be \(\in\) \(\mathscr{F}\), which is contrary to the choice of the sequence of the \(X_i\). This implies that \(U\) is connected.

(i) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (ii). Let \(Y=\bigcup_{X', X''} X'\cap X''\) where one requires that \(X'\) and \(X''\) be two distinct irreducible components of \(X\) such that \(X'\cap X'' \in \mathscr{F}\). The family of the \(X'\cap X''\) is locally finite since \(X\) is locally noetherian, moreover the \(X'\cap X''\) are closed, hence \(Y\) is closed. Furthermore, \(Y\) belongs locally to \(\mathscr{F}\), hence \(Y\in \mathscr{F}\). Hence \(U=X-Y\) is connected. Let \(X'\) and \(X''\) be two distinct irreducible components of \(X\), let \(U'\) and \(U''\) be their traces on \(U\), which are nonempty by construction of \(Y\). These are irreducible components of \(U\), now \(U\) is connected, hence, \(U\) being locally noetherian, there exists a sequence of irreducible components \(U_0, \ldots, U_n\) of \(U\), such that \(U_0=U'\), \(U_n=U''\) and \(U_{i}\cap U_{i+1}\neq \emptyset\) and \(\neq U_{i}, 0\leqslant i < n\). Let \(X_0, \ldots, X_n\) be the sequence of irreducible components of \(X\) which is such that \(X_{i}\cap U=U_{i}\); if \(X_{i}\cap X_{i+1}\in \mathscr{F}\), by construction of \(\mathscr{F}\), \(U_{i}\cap U_{i+1}=\emptyset\) or \(U_{i}=U_{i+1}\) which is not possible by the choice of the \(U_{i}\).

Q.E.D.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.9

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring. Suppose that for every prime ideal \(\mathfrak{p}\) of \(A\), we have:

\[ (\dim A_\mathfrak{p} \geqslant 2) \mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow} (\operatorname{prof} A_\mathfrak{p} \geqslant 2) \]

Suppose moreover that \(A\) satisfies the chain condition1. Then, for every \(\mathfrak{p}\), minimal prime ideal of \(A\), \(\dim A/\mathfrak{p} =\dim A\), or again, all the irreducible components of \(\operatorname{Spec} A\) have the same dimension: that of \(A\).

If \(X'\) and \(X''\) are two irreducible components of \(X\), one joins them by a chain having the properties enumerated in 3.7; it then suffices to prove that two successive components have the same dimension, which follows from the second hypothesis.

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example 3.10

Let \(X\) be the union of two complementary vector subspaces of respective dimensions \(2\) and \(3\) in a vector space of dimension \(5\); more precisely, let \(X=\operatorname{Spec} A\), with \(A=B/{\mathfrak{p} \cap \mathfrak{q}}\), where \(B=k[X_1, \ldots, X_5]\), \(\mathfrak{p}\) is the ideal generated by \(X_1, X_2, X_3\) and \(\mathfrak{q}\) the ideal generated by \(X_4\) and \(X_5\); \(X\) can be disconnected by the point of intersection \(x\) of the two vector subspaces, hence the depth of \(\mathscr{O}_{X, x}\) is equal to \(1\), since it cannot be \(\geqslant 2\) by virtue of Theorem 3.6. Another reason: the equidimensionality conclusion of the preceding corollary fails.

More generally, taking a union \(X\) of two vector subspaces of dimension \(p, q\geqslant 2\) in a vector space of dimension \(p+q\), for no embedding of \(X\) into a regular scheme is \(X\) even set-theoretically a complete intersection at the origin, since (up to modifying it without changing the underlying topological space in a neighbourhood of the origin), \(X\) would be Cohen-Macaulay hence of depth \(\geqslant 2\) at the origin, which is not the case.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 3.11

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme, \(Y\) a closed subprescheme of \(X\), \(F\) an \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module. Depth is a purely topological notion which is expressed in terms of the vanishing of the \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y^i(X, F)\) for \(i<n\). One also wishes to study these sheaves for a given \(i\), or for \(i>n\). In this connection one proves the following result:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.12

Let \(m\) be an integer; for \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y^i(X, F)=0\), \(i>m\) for every coherent \(F\), it is necessary and sufficient that this hold for \(F=\mathscr{O}_X\).

By inductive limit it is then true for every quasi-coherent sheaf. For example, if \(Y\) can be described locally by \(m\) equations, or, as one says, if \(Y\) is locally set-theoretically a complete intersection (which happens for example if \(X\) and \(Y\) are nonsingular) it follows from the computation of the \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y^i(X, F)\) by the Koszul complex that these sheaves are zero for \(i>m\). This fact has moreover been used implicitly in Example 3.10. This cohomological condition is however not sufficient, as the following example proves:

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example 3.13

Let \(X=\operatorname{Spec} (A)\), where \(A\) is a noetherian local ring, normal, of dimension \(2\). Let \(Y\) be a curve in \(X\). One can prove that the complement of the curve is an affine open set, hence \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y^i(X, \mathscr{O}_X) \approx \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y^i(X, \mathscr{O}_X)\) \(\operatorname{H}^{i-1}(X-Y, \mathscr{O}_X)=0\). However one can construct a curve which is not described by an equation.

We shall seek2 conditions for the \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y^i(X, F)\) to be coherent for a given \(i\), which is not the case in general, as obvious examples show, for instance \(\operatorname{H}_\mathfrak{m}^n(A)\) for \(A\) a noetherian local ring of dimension \(n>0\); when for example \(A\) is a discrete valuation ring with field of fractions \(K\), one finds \(\operatorname{H}_\mathfrak{m}^1(A) \simeq K/A\), which is not a finitely generated module over \(A\). To enlighten the reader, let us say that the problem posed is equivalent to the following: Let \(f:U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) be an open immersion, let \(G\) be a coherent sheaf on \(U\), find criteria for the higher direct images \(\operatorname{R}^i f_* G\) to be coherent sheaves on \(X\) for a given \(i\). These conditions are necessary for the use of formal geometry that we have seen in Exp IX and following.
